one_line_takeaway: Sentra-Guard fuses SBERT-FAISS retrieval, a fine-tuned transformer classifier, and zero-shot NLI entailment behind a HITL-updatable FAISS index to claim 99.98% accuracy and 0.004% ASR at 47ms on HarmBench-28K, with the paper's own limitations section flagging unresolved Unicode-homoglyph and generation-time (multi-turn) gaps.
key_finding: On the primary D1 test set (24,145 adversarial prompts), Sentra-Guard reports 99.98% accuracy, 100.00% precision, 99.97% recall, F1=99.98%, AUC=1.00, and ASR=0.004% at 47ms average latency, versus baselines evaluated under identical conditions: Ensemble Filter (92.83% acc, 598ms), Zero-Shot Only/BART-MNLI (88.76% acc, 385ms), and Static Keyword Filter (75.02% acc, 63ms). Error analysis of the confusion matrix: exactly 1 false negative (a Unicode homoglyph variant) and 7 false positives (benign prompts with technical terms like 'bomb calorimeter') out of 24,145 prompts. Cross-lingual results (Table V) show baseline (undefended) LLM ASR ranging 78.5%-93.5% across language/model pairs, with Sentra-Guard's detection rate (DR) ranging 94.3% (Mistral 7B, Arabic) to 99.5% (Claude 3 Opus, English), FPR below 2.1%, and latency 42-56ms. Cross-dataset generalization (Table VI) held accuracy above 98.8% on all four external sets, with the JailbreakTracer Corpus being the hardest (98.88% acc, ASR 0.012%). The HITL Vector Injection mechanism: injecting 500 newly confirmed adversarial prompts improved recall by 4.2% and reduced false positives by 11% without retraining, and reduced per-update adaptation latency from the 2-4 hours needed to backpropagate through DeBERTa-v3's ~184M parameters to under 2 minutes for a single vector insertion into the FAISS index.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Sentra-Guard, a modular real-time defense system for LLM jailbreak/prompt-injection detection that fuses three parallel branches on a translated, normalized prompt: (1) SBERT-embedding + FAISS semantic-retrieval similarity against a knowledge base of known harmful/safe exemplars, (2) a fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3/DistilBERT transformer classifier, and (3) a zero-shot NLI entailment classifier (facebook/bart-large-mnli). A weighted decision-fusion layer combines the three branch scores into a final risk score; low-confidence or disagreeing cases are escalated to a human-in-the-loop (HITL) reviewer. Confirmed-harmful HITL decisions are inserted directly as new vectors into the FAISS index ('Vector Injection') rather than triggering full model retraining, which the authors claim reduces adaptation lag by over 90% relative to backpropagating through the ~184M-parameter DeBERTa-v3 backbone (2-4 hours vs. under 2 minutes). A language-agnostic normalization/translation layer maps 100+ languages to English before the pipeline runs, targeting multilingual and code-mixed obfuscation attacks.
threat_model: Black-box adversary who interacts with the target LLM only through natural-language prompts and observes only the final allowed/blocked response, with no access to classifier weights or the FAISS-indexed knowledge base (Dadv). Adversary goal: bypass LLM safety alignment to elicit prohibited content (illegal-activity instructions, hate speech, extraction of sensitive training data). Attack vectors explicitly considered: multilingual/code-mixed obfuscation, role-play and meta-prompting (fictional/layered framing), and semantic paraphrase/logical-misdirection attacks that preserve harmful intent while altering surface syntax. The NMT engine and SBERT encoder are treated as trusted components; Unicode homoglyph/script-manipulation attempts are explicitly named as a known gap addressed only reactively via HITL, not by the preprocessing pipeline design itself (contradicting a claim earlier in the same section that the pipeline 'addresses' such attempts -- Section VII.F later concedes 'the system lacks a dedicated homoglyph resolution stage').
limitations: The paper's own Section VII.F lists: (1) no dedicated Unicode homoglyph resolution stage (character substitutions can push retrieval similarity below threshold -- this is exactly the single false negative observed); (2) NMT quality for low-resource/heavily code-mixed languages may not preserve adversarial semantic intent; (3) the fusion decision threshold theta_A was tuned on D1 and may not generalize to deployments with different adversarial distributions; (4) the framework is text-only and does not address multimodal (vision/audio) attack surfaces; (5) prompts are evaluated only at fixed pipeline checkpoints, not during token generation, limiting coverage of multi-turn crescendo attacks despite crescendo being one of the eight attack types the system is evaluated against; (6) benchmark results may not transfer to live production traffic without longitudinal evaluation. Beyond the authors' own list: the framework-level comparison to JailbreakTracer/LLM-Sentry/JBShield (Table IV, Table VI) is not an apples-to-apples reproduction -- those numbers come from each system's own paper on different, non-unified datasets, which the authors themselves flag but the headline '20-fold ASR reduction' claim in the Discussion nonetheless treats it as a direct comparison. The near-perfect metrics on the authors' own primary benchmark (100.00% precision, AUC=1.00, F1=1.00 sustained across all recall thresholds) are unusually strong for an adversarial detection task and, combined with D1 being a benchmark the authors themselves curated/split, warrant caution about possible train/test leakage or benchmark-specific overfitting that the paper does not itself investigate.

datasets_used: HarmBench-28K (D1, primary training/test corpus, 70/15/15 stratified split)
Supplementary corpus D2 (public red-teaming repositories, inference-only)
JBB-Behaviors, split into Harmful and Benign subsets (external cross-dataset generalization)
quantitative_results: D1 test set (24,145 prompts): accuracy 99.98%, precision 100.00%, recall 99.97%, F1 99.98%, AUC 1.00, ASR 0.004%, avg latency 47ms; 1 false negative, 7 false positives
Baselines on D1: Static Keyword Filter 75.02% acc / 63ms; Zero-Shot Only (BART-MNLI) 88.76% acc / 385ms; Ensemble Filter 92.83% acc / 598ms

Cross-lingual (Table V), undefended baseline ASR 78.5%-93.5% across GPT-4o/Gemini Flash/Claude 3 Opus/Mistral 7B x English/French/Spanish/Arabic/Hindi; Sentra-Guard detection rate 94.3% (Mistral 7B, Arabic, worst) to 99.5% (Claude 3 Opus, English, best); FPR <=2.1%; latency 42-56ms

Cross-dataset (Table VI): JailbreakV-28K 99.91% acc / ASR 0.009%; JBB-Behaviors Harmful 99.94% acc / ASR 0.007%; JBB-Behaviors Benign 99.96% acc / 0 false positives; JailbreakTracer Corpus 98.88% acc / ASR 0.012%

HITL Vector Injection: 500 injected adversarial prompts -> +4.2% recall, -11% false positives, no retraining; adaptation latency 2-4 hours (full DeBERTa-v3 retrain, ~184M params) reduced to <2 minutes
Robustness across 8 catalogued jailbreak strategies: >98.7% of attempts neutralized overall; meta-prompting specifically detected at 97.9%

ROC/AUC comparison figure claims: Sentra-Guard AUC 1.00 vs OpenAI Moderation 0.987, Vigil 0.992, NeMo Guardrails 0.984 (these comparator AUCs are cited from other systems' reported figures, not reproduced by the authors)
baselines_compared: Static Keyword Filter (author-implemented, evaluated on D1 under identical conditions)
Zero-Shot Classifier / BART-MNLI alone (author-implemented, evaluated on D1)
Ensemble Moderation Pipeline (author-implemented, evaluated on D1)
JailbreakTracer [36], LLM-Sentry [37], JBShield [38] -- numbers taken from each system's own publication, not reproduced on a unified dataset (paper's own caveat)
OpenAI Moderation, Vigil, NeMo Guardrails -- cited AUC figures only, source unspecified/likely from those systems' own reports
